name: Commercial Invoice for Export
summary: Drafts a precise Commercial Invoice for Export, a key regulatory document essential for international trade compliance, customs clearance, and payment processing under letters of credit. Extracts and ensures consistency of critical details like party identifications, product specifications, pricing, Incoterms, and regulatory requirements from source documents to prevent shipment delays, penalties, or payment rejections. Use this skill when preparing export documentation for multi-jurisdictional scrutiny by customs, banks, and logistics providers.
content:
# Commercial Invoice for Export - Enhanced Professional Legal Workflow

## Document Purpose and Regulatory Framework

You are tasked with drafting a Commercial Invoice for Export, a mission-critical regulatory document that serves as the cornerstone of international trade compliance. This invoice functions simultaneously as a commercial record of the transaction, a customs declaration document, and a financial instrument for payment processing. The document will be scrutinized by customs authorities in both the exporting and importing countries for tariff classification and duty assessment, reviewed by freight forwarders and logistics providers for shipping coordination, examined by financial institutions for letter of credit compliance and payment release, and relied upon by insurance underwriters for coverage verification and claims processing. Given this multi-jurisdictional, multi-stakeholder reliance, absolute precision, completeness, and regulatory compliance are non-negotiable requirements. Any inaccuracy, omission, or inconsistency can trigger cascading consequences including shipment detention at customs, financial penalties ranging from administrative fines to criminal liability for false declarations, rejection of the entire shipment requiring re-export at the shipper's expense, documentary discrepancies that jeopardize payment under letters of credit, and potential debarment from export privileges.

Before commencing the drafting process, conduct a comprehensive search through all uploaded documents, correspondence, and transaction files to identify and extract relevant information. You should locate and analyze purchase orders, proforma invoices, letters of credit, sales contracts, previous commercial invoices for similar transactions, email correspondence containing agreed terms, shipping instructions, export licenses, and any regulatory compliance documentation. From these source materials, extract with precision the complete legal names of parties, their registered addresses and tax identification numbers, detailed product specifications including technical parameters and model numbers, agreed pricing structures and payment terms, specified Incoterms and delivery obligations, and any special conditions or requirements imposed by the buyer, financial institutions, or regulatory authorities. Ensure absolute consistency between the commercial invoice and all underlying transaction documents, as discrepancies—even minor variations in product descriptions or pricing—can result in letter of credit rejection or customs challenges.

## Party Identification with Legal Precision

Draft the exporter and importer identification sections with meticulous attention to legal entity accuracy and jurisdictional requirements. For the shipper or exporter, you must provide the complete legal business name exactly as registered with the relevant commercial registry, corporate authority, or business licensing body in the country of export. This is not a trade name, doing-business-as name, or abbreviated version unless that abbreviated form is the actual registered legal name. Include the full registered business address with street number and name, suite or unit designation if applicable, city, state or province with proper designation, postal or ZIP code in the correct format, and country name spelled out completely. You must specify all relevant tax and regulatory identification numbers including the tax identification number, employer identification number, value-added tax registration number, export registration number, or any other identifier required by the exporting country's customs and tax authorities. Include comprehensive contact information with telephone number in international format including country code and area code, a monitored email address designated for customs and shipping inquiries, and if applicable, the name and title of a specific contact person authorized to respond to regulatory queries. This exporter information must match precisely—character for character—with the information appearing on export licenses, shipper's export declarations, automated export system filings, and any other regulatory submissions to avoid triggering compliance alerts or red flags during customs processing.

For the consignee or importer, provide identical levels of detail and precision using the full legal business name as it appears in the destination country's commercial registry or business licensing records. The consignee address must be the actual delivery location or customs clearance location, complete and accurate down to the specific building designation, and must include any special location identifiers such as free trade zone designations, bonded warehouse facility codes, or special economic zone indicators that affect customs processing and duty liability. Include the importer's tax identification number in the format required by the destination country, which may be a VAT number in European Union countries, a business number in Canada, an employer identification number in the United States, or other jurisdiction-specific identifiers. You must also include any import license numbers, importer of record numbers, or customs broker references if the destination country requires such information for clearance. Designate an authorized contact person with full name, official title, direct telephone number, and email address who has authority to respond to customs queries, resolve documentary issues, or make decisions regarding customs clearance. Verify with absolute certainty that the consignee information aligns perfectly with the information appearing in purchase orders, letters of credit, import permits, and any other transaction documents, as even minor discrepancies in spelling, address format, or identification numbers can trigger payment delays, customs holds, or documentary rejection.

## Transaction Reference and Commercial Terms

Generate a unique invoice number following your organization's established export invoice numbering system, ensuring the number is sequential, non-duplicative, and traceable through your export compliance and accounting systems. The invoice numbering system should enable immediate identification of the transaction year, sequential position, and potentially the destination country or product category if your system incorporates such coding. Include the complete date of invoice issuance in the format required or customary in the destination country—typically DD/MM/YYYY format for most international markets including European, Asian, and Latin American destinations, or MM/DD/YYYY format for transactions involving the United States. This invoice number serves as the master reference that must appear consistently across all related shipping and regulatory documents including the bill of lading, air waybill, packing list, certificate of origin, insurance certificate, inspection certificate, and any export licenses or declarations. The invoice number must be traceable in your internal systems for audit purposes, as export control authorities typically require the ability to reconstruct complete transaction records for five to seven years following export.

Specify with absolute clarity the applicable Incoterms 2020 rule (or the current version in effect at the time of transaction) that governs the precise allocation of costs, risks, and responsibilities between buyer and seller. You must state the three-letter Incoterm abbreviation followed by the complete named place or port as required by the chosen rule. For example, "FOB (Free on Board) Shanghai Port, China" or "DDP (Delivered Duty Paid) 123 Industrial Boulevard, Toronto, Ontario M1M 1M1, Canada" or "CIF (Cost, Insurance and Freight) Port of Hamburg, Germany." The chosen Incoterm determines the exact point at which risk of loss or damage transfers from seller to buyer, who bears responsibility for export customs clearance, who arranges and pays for main carriage, who procures and pays for cargo insurance, who handles import customs clearance, and who pays import duties and taxes. This designation directly affects the invoice valuation, as the unit prices and total value must reflect all costs included under the specified Incoterm. If the transaction involves any additional shipping terms, special payment arrangements for specific cost components, or deviations from standard Incoterm allocations, state these explicitly and unambiguously to prevent disputes and ensure all parties understand their obligations.

## Product Description and Valuation with Customs Compliance

Provide a comprehensive and technically precise description of the goods being exported that enables customs officials in both the exporting and importing countries to identify, classify, and value the merchandise without ambiguity, additional inquiry, or physical inspection beyond standard verification procedures. The product description must include the commercial name by which the product is known in international trade, complete technical specifications such as dimensions with units clearly stated, capacity or volume, performance characteristics or ratings, materials of construction or composition with percentages if the product is a mixture or alloy, manufacturing process if relevant to classification, intended use or application in sufficient detail to distinguish the product from similar items, manufacturer's model numbers or part numbers, brand names and trademarks if applicable, and any other distinguishing characteristics that differentiate these specific goods from similar products that might fall under different tariff classifications. You must avoid vague, generic, or ambiguous terms such as "machine parts," "electronic equipment," "chemical products," or "textile goods" and instead provide specific detail such as "stainless steel ball bearings, grade 316L, outer diameter 25mm, inner diameter 10mm, width 7mm, precision class ABEC-7, for industrial machinery applications" or "men's woven shirts, 100% cotton poplin fabric, long sleeves, button-front closure, sizes M-XXL, casual style." The description must match exactly the description appearing in the harmonized tariff schedule classification, any export licenses or controlled goods declarations, and the underlying commercial documents such as purchase orders and proforma invoices.

Specify the precise quantity of goods using appropriate units of measurement that align with international trade practices for the product category and the requirements of the destination country's customs authority. State quantities in metric units (kilograms, metric tons, liters, cubic meters, square meters, linear meters) unless the destination country specifically requires or customarily uses imperial units. Include both the number of shipping units (packages, cartons, crates, pallets, containers) and the total quantity of individual items or measurement units. For example, "5,000 pieces packed in 100 cartons" or "2,500 kilograms net weight in 50 bags" provides clarity for both customs valuation purposes and physical verification during inspection. If the product is sold by weight, volume, or area, ensure the quantity stated corresponds to the pricing basis.

State the unit price in the agreed currency of transaction, ensuring it reflects the actual commercial value agreed between buyer and seller and corresponds precisely to the cost allocation under the specified Incoterms. If the Incoterm is EXW (Ex Works), the unit price reflects only the value of goods at the factory gate; if FOB, it includes the value of goods plus all costs to load them on board the vessel; if CIF, it includes cost of goods, international freight, and marine insurance to the destination port; if DDP, it includes all costs through to delivery at the named destination including import duties and taxes. The unit pricing must be consistent with any proforma invoices previously issued, purchase orders executed, sales contracts signed, and letters of credit opened, as discrepancies will create documentary problems that can result in payment refusal by banks or challenges by customs authorities investigating potential undervaluation.

Calculate and clearly state the total value for each line item by multiplying quantity by unit price, showing the calculation if space permits or if required by destination country regulations. Provide subtotals for product categories if the invoice includes multiple types of goods, and provide the overall invoice total representing the sum of all line items. Ensure absolute mathematical accuracy, as calculation errors will be detected during customs processing and bank document examination and may raise questions about the invoice's reliability or suggest intentional misrepresentation. The total invoice value should reflect all costs included under the specified Incoterms and must be stated in the transaction currency. If freight, insurance, or other costs are itemized separately, clearly indicate whether these amounts are included in or additional to the merchandise value. This total value figure will be used by customs authorities for valuation and duty assessment, by insurance companies for determining coverage limits and premium calculations, by banks for verifying letter of credit compliance and authorizing payment, and by your own organization for revenue recognition and export reporting.

## Origin Determination and Tariff Classification Compliance

Identify and declare the country of origin for the goods with precision, understanding that country of origin is a legal determination based on where the products were manufactured, produced, or underwent their last substantial transformation as defined by the applicable rules of origin. This is not simply the country where the exporter is located or where the goods were purchased, but rather the country where the goods acquired their essential character and commercial identity. For products manufactured entirely in a single country from materials originating in that same country, the determination is straightforward. However, for goods involving multi-country production, assembly operations, or significant imported content, you must apply the appropriate origin rules, which may be based on tariff classification shift (the product is in a different tariff heading than the inputs), value-added thresholds (a specified percentage of value was added in the country of origin), regional value content calculations, or specific manufacturing or processing operations that confer origin. The country of origin determination directly affects the applicable duty rates in the destination country, eligibility for tariff rate quotas, qualification for preferential tariff treatment under free trade agreements, compliance with country-specific import restrictions or prohibitions, and satisfaction of government procurement preferences or trade remedy measures such as anti-dumping or countervailing duties.

If the transaction is intended to benefit from preferential tariff treatment under a free trade agreement such as USMCA (United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement), CPTPP (Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership), EU trade agreements, ASEAN agreements, or bilateral free trade agreements, ensure the origin determination is supported by appropriate documentation and that the goods genuinely qualify under the specific rules of origin established by that agreement. You may need to reference a certificate of origin, a declaration of origin, or origin certification documentation, and you should verify that the exporter holds any required certifications or registrations as an approved exporter under the relevant trade agreement.

Provide the Harmonized Tariff Schedule code or Harmonized System code that classifies the goods according to international customs nomenclature, using the complete code required by the destination country. The international Harmonized System uses six-digit codes that are uniform globally, but most countries extend these to eight, ten, or even more digits for national tariff purposes, with each additional digit providing more specific classification and potentially different duty rates, import restrictions, or statistical tracking requirements. Accurate classification is absolutely critical as it determines the applicable duty rate that the importer must pay, identifies any import restrictions such as licensing requirements or quotas, triggers any special trade remedy duties such as anti-dumping or countervailing duties, determines eligibility for preferential tariff treatment, and ensures proper statistical tracking for trade data purposes. You must verify the tariff code against the current tariff schedule of the destination country, recognizing that tariff codes, duty rates, and trade remedy measures change periodically through legislative action, trade negotiations, or administrative determinations. For complex products, products that could fall under multiple plausible classifications, or high-value shipments where classification significantly affects duty liability, you should consider consulting with a licensed customs broker in the destination country, engaging a tariff classification specialist, or requesting a binding tariff ruling from the destination country's customs authority to ensure classification certainty and protect against reclassification penalties.

## Certification, Authentication, and Legal Accountability

Include a comprehensive declaration statement that certifies the accuracy and completeness of all information provided in the commercial invoice and creates legal responsibility and potential liability for the representations made. The declaration should be prominently placed and should read: "I hereby declare and certify that all information contained in this commercial invoice is true, correct, complete, and accurate to the best of my knowledge, information, and belief. The goods described herein are of the country of origin stated, and the prices shown represent the actual transaction value agreed between buyer and seller without any hidden or undisclosed considerations. The quantities, specifications, and values stated are accurate and verifiable. This invoice is issued in full compliance with all applicable export control regulations, trade laws, economic sanctions requirements, and anti-boycott regulations of the exporting country, and to the best of my knowledge and belief complies with the import regulations and customs requirements of the destination country. I understand that civil and criminal penalties may be imposed for making false or fraudulent statements or for the violation of any export control laws or regulations."

Provide clearly designated space for an authorized signature by a company officer, export compliance manager, or designated representative who has express authority to bind the company in international trade matters and who can be held personally and corporately accountable for the accuracy of the information and compliance with applicable laws. Include separate lines for the handwritten signature, printed name in full, official title within the organization, and date of signature in the format appropriate for the jurisdiction. The signature authenticates the document, creates legal responsibility for any misrepresentations or false statements, and may expose the signatory to personal liability for violations of export control laws, customs fraud statutes, or sanctions regulations. In jurisdictions requiring company seals, official stamps, notarization, or legalization by chambers of commerce or consular offices, ensure appropriate space is allocated for such authentication elements and include a notation of any such requirements based on the destination country's regulations or letter of credit stipulations.

## Supplementary Compliance Information and Documentary Coordination

Ensure the commercial invoice includes all mandatory elements required by the export control regulations of the exporting country and the customs regulations of the importing country, recognizing that these requirements vary significantly by jurisdiction, product type, and transaction value. Cross-reference the invoice number with the bill of lading number or air waybill number, packing list reference number, certificate of origin number, and any inspection certificate numbers to maintain documentary consistency and enable customs officials and other stakeholders to match related documents and verify that all documents describe the same transaction. If the transaction is governed by a letter of credit, you must verify with absolute precision that all invoice details including description of goods, quantity specifications, unit prices, total value, shipping terms, latest shipment date, and any special notations required by the credit terms strictly comply with the letter of credit requirements to avoid discrepancies that could result in the bank refusing payment or requiring amendment of the credit.

Consider and include additional information elements that may be required for specific products, destinations, regulatory regimes, or transaction types. For controlled goods subject to export licensing under the Export Administration Regulations, International Traffic in Arms Regulations, or equivalent export control frameworks, include the export license number, license expiration date, and the specific Export Control Classification Number or United States Munitions List category. If a certificate of origin has been issued, reference the certificate number, date of issuance, and issuing authority. For shipments requiring cargo insurance, include the insurance policy number, name of the insurance company, and coverage amount. Include shipping marks, container numbers, and seal numbers that correspond exactly to the packing list and bill of lading to enable physical verification. Specify both net weight (weight of goods only, excluding all packaging materials) and gross weight (total weight including all packaging materials, pallets, and containers) in kilograms or other appropriate units, as many countries require both figures for customs clearance, duty assessment, and freight calculation purposes. If the goods require special handling, temperature-controlled transport, or have hazardous materials classifications under the International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code or equivalent air transport regulations, include appropriate warnings, handling instructions, and regulatory classification codes.

Format the document in a professional manner with clear section headings, consistent and readable fonts, logical information flow that facilitates review by customs officials and other stakeholders, and sufficient white space to prevent crowding and ensure readability. Use official company letterhead if available, or ensure company identification including logo, full legal name, and contact information is prominent at the top of the document. The document should project professionalism and attention to detail, as the quality of presentation can influence how seriously customs officials and banks treat the document. Maintain copies of all commercial invoices and all supporting documentation for the period required by export recordkeeping regulations, which is typically five years in the United States under Export Administration Regulations and can range from five to ten years in other jurisdictions. These records must be maintained in a manner that makes them readily available for government audit, and may be required to support warranty claims, insurance claims, dispute resolution, or investigations of alleged violations.

If any information required for the commercial invoice is not available in the uploaded documents, cannot be determined from the transaction context, or requires clarification, you must identify the specific missing information with precision, explain why it is required and what consequences may result from its omission, and request it from the user before completing the invoice to ensure the document is complete, compliant, and effective upon first issuance without need for correction or amendment.
